caveats: a: This residual NAICS code mixes software-centric and asset-intensive operating models.
a: The broad occupation source is dominated by telecommunications carriers classified outside the industry.
rho: The vendor survey is global and not specific to this six-digit industry or target firm size.
rho: Assessment or pilot activity does not establish implemented labor substitution.
e: The dataset does not provide a defensible firm count for this industry, so absolute eligible-firm availability remains unknown.
e: Public VoIP company evidence does not establish the composition of the residual NAICS population.
s5: The succession survey covers employer businesses across all industries.
s5: Telecom deal-flow evidence includes large carriers, infrastructure assets, and foreign transactions outside the lens.
q: Recurring revenue is not a direct measure of retained automation benefit.
q: Satellite tracking and radar contracts may retain savings differently from VoIP and Internet services.
d5: No public source measures constant-price, constant-quality demand for the combined residual NAICS basket.
d5: The component service lines have materially different demand trajectories.
o: The estimate averages service models with very different substitution risk.
o: It measures continued need for a lens-type operator, not retention by the current provider.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers pay subscriptions, usage charges, managed-service fees, or repeat operating contracts for voice, connectivity, monitoring, telemetry, tracking, and related communications operations.
lens.excluded_activities: Telecommunications carriers classified elsewhere, captive internal units, shell entities, software vendors that do not operate a communications service, one-time equipment sales, and businesses outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat non-carrier telecommunications services to external customers, including customer-connection Internet and VoIP services, satellite terminal and tracking operations, communications telemetry, and radar-station operation.

a.bridge: BLS measures three-digit NAICS 517 rather than this residual industry: installation and repair is 29.67% of employment, office and administrative support is 14.85%, sales is 17.82%, and computer and mathematical work is 16.04%. The estimate maps support, billing, sales-assistance, monitoring, documentation, and software tasks to AI exposure while preserving human physical work and accountable technical decisions. Carrier-heavy BLS employment probably overstates field installation for software-centric VoIP firms but may understate it for tracking and terminal operations, so the net error direction is indeterminate.
caveats: This residual NAICS code mixes software-centric and asset-intensive operating models.
The broad occupation source is dominated by telecommunications carriers classified outside the industry.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census across independent VoIP, customer-connection Internet, satellite terminal, tracking, telemetry, and radar operators, including current automation status.
a.rationale: AI can handle portions of ticketing, customer support, billing, fraud review, documentation, sales support, call-flow configuration, telemetry triage, and incident summarization. Physical station work, network changes, emergency response, and high-consequence technical decisions remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: FCC reported 63 million interconnected VoIP subscriptions in June 2025 and a 2.3% compound annual decline over its reported three-year period, while business connections remained the majority of wireline voice. SIA reported strong satellite broadband and ground-network growth in 2025, which supports tracking, terminal, and telemetry workloads. The estimate combines opposing indicators for the industry's mixed service basket and converts nominal revenue or subscription evidence to five-year constant-quality quantity with a wide band. VoIP data may understate demand in satellite operations, while global satellite growth may overstate US residual-industry demand.
caveats: No public source measures constant-price, constant-quality demand for the combined residual NAICS basket.
The component service lines have materially different demand trajectories.
d5.change_evidence: Separate US quantity series for customer-connection Internet accounts, VoIP subscriptions and traffic, active satellite terminals, tracking hours, telemetry volume, and radar operating contracts, reconciled to industry receipts.
d5.rationale: Legacy dial-up and standalone voice face contraction, and mature VoIP lines can be displaced by collaboration suites. Business communications, programmable voice, satellite terminal operations, telemetry, tracking, and growing orbital activity provide offsetting demand.

caveats: The dataset does not provide a defensible firm count for this industry, so absolute eligible-firm availability remains unknown.
Public VoIP company evidence does not establish the composition of the residual NAICS population.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level business register matched to ownership, service mix, external-customer status, recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, headcount, and active operating status.
e.rationale: Most defined activities are external services capable of recurring or repeat revenue, but eligibility is reduced by very small owner-operated providers, legacy dial-up businesses, captive station units, software-only vendors, one-time project contractors, and firms outside the earnings band. The estimate is conditional on a firm being in the target size population; it does not replace the unavailable dataset firm count.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 517 ($71,420,936,579) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 517 ($668,853,509,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 517. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3470 (margins.json[517]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Telecom. Services EBITDA/Sales 34.70%); revenue/employee $711,427 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 517); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate averages service models with very different substitution risk.
It measures continued need for a lens-type operator, not retention by the current provider.
o.change_evidence: Customer churn and win-loss studies attributing departures to self-service, bundled collaboration software, carrier integration, software-only substitutes, or continued outsourced operation, segmented by service model.
o.rationale: VoIP, connectivity, tracking, telemetry, and radar services require an accountable provider to operate platforms or stations, manage routing and availability, satisfy regulatory duties, and resolve incidents. Some voice and Internet demand can migrate to bundled software, self-service, or facilities-based carriers, while specialized station operations remain operator-intensive.

q.bridge: Bandwidth reported that the majority of cloud-communications revenue is generated from recurring usage and monthly fees, with $146.9 million of recurring cloud-communications fees in 2025. FCC data also show a subscription-based VoIP market dominated by non-ILECs. The estimate treats subscriptions and embedded communication workflows as supportive of retention, then discounts for usage pricing, carrier pass-through charges, renewal repricing, intense platform competition, and the code's non-VoIP services. A scaled cloud communications platform may have more software differentiation than a typical provider and therefore may overstate retention.
caveats: Recurring revenue is not a direct measure of retained automation benefit.
Satellite tracking and radar contracts may retain savings differently from VoIP and Internet services.
q.change_evidence: Representative private-company contract and cohort data showing fixed, usage, and cost-pass-through billing; renewal repricing; gross retention; customer concentration; and margin movement after automation.
q.rationale: Fixed subscriptions and managed contracts can retain part of implemented savings, but per-minute billing, wholesale inputs, transparent alternatives, contract renewals, and customer bargaining transmit a substantial share through price or service expansion.

rho.bridge: NVIDIA surveyed more than 450 global telecommunications professionals: 40% reported deploying AI in network planning and operations, 77% reported operating-cost reductions, and almost all were deploying or assessing AI. The estimate translates current adoption and perceived impact into five-year implementation of exposed work, discounting for lower-middle-market integration limits and for safety and reliability constraints in station operations. Large telecom respondents probably overstate implementation capacity for small operators.
caveats: The vendor survey is global and not specific to this six-digit industry or target firm size.
Assessment or pilot activity does not establish implemented labor substitution.
rho.change_evidence: A US operator survey reporting production AI use by workflow, labor hours affected, exception rates, compliance controls, and realized headcount avoidance for each major business model in this code.
rho.rationale: Cloud communications workflows can adopt mature AI tooling quickly, while legacy platforms, fragmented customer configurations, limited telemetry labels, compliance, uptime obligations, and physical operations constrain full implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while Deloitte expected roughly 400 telecom transactions in 2025 and emphasized consolidation. The estimate starts near the employer-owner intention rate, discounts plans that fail or do not qualify as control transfers, and allows for sector acquisition activity. Owner intentions probably overstate completed transfers, while large telecom transactions may overstate lower-middle-market activity.
caveats: The succession survey covers employer businesses across all industries.
Telecom deal-flow evidence includes large carriers, infrastructure assets, and foreign transactions outside the lens.
s5.change_evidence: A US transaction database for firms classified in NAICS 517810, matched to revenue, earnings, business model, seller ownership age, control status, and failed sale processes.
s5.rationale: Recurring customer relationships, licenses, platforms, and technical teams can attract buyers, but owner dependence, customer concentration, weak legacy assets, small scale, and regulatory approvals reduce completion probability.

principal_driver: Recurring digital communications and specialized operations both contain automatable information workflows while preserving an accountable service-provider role.
principal_weakness: The missing firm-count input and the residual code's incompatible business models prevent a confident view of absolute transferable-firm availability.
